results.title: Self-Evolving Rubrics for Dynamic Reward Modeling
results.content:
A self-evolving rubric is an adaptive, structured set of evaluation or reward criteria that is automatically generated, iteratively refined, and continually adapted in response to evolving model outputs and new task demands. Unlike static rubrics—which are fixed, typically human-authored, and prone to coverage gaps or obsolescence—self-evolving rubrics exploit algorithmic or model-driven cycles to generate criteria that capture both explicit constraints (“hard rules”) and implicit principles, align dynamically with emerging behaviors, and drive fine-grained, interpretable, and robust supervision for reward modeling, evaluation, and alignment of LLMs (Liu et al., 9 Oct 2025).

## 1. Contrastive Rubric Generation (CRG): Methodology and Criteria Discovery [...] 2000 character limit reached

# Self-Evolving Rubrics for Dynamic Reward Modeling

Updated 13 February 2026

 Self-evolving rubrics are adaptive evaluation criteria that iteratively generate and refine rules based on evolving model outputs and task demands.
 They employ contrastive rubric generation, margin-based optimization, and preference-label filtering to ensure discriminative and robust evaluation.
 By dynamically adapting to model behavior, self-evolving rubrics overcome static rubric limitations and enhance reward modeling for alignment. [...] Future Directions:

 Closed-loop human-in-the-loop refinement, leveraging online adaptation to correct or augment rubrics as new behaviors arise.
 Broadening coverage to open-ended generation, multi-modal tasks, or domains without verifiable labels.
 Research into automated stopping criteria and dynamic complexity control for rubric sets.
 Integration with other principle-driven alignment strategies for seamless task adaptation.

Self-evolving rubrics thus represent a flexible, modular, and scalable alternative to scalar reward supervision—enabling LLM training regimes that autonomously track, diagnose, and enforce emerging aspects of quality and alignment as model populations advance.

results.title: Reinforcing Chain-of-Thought Reasoning with Self-Evolving Rubrics
results.content:
### 4.4 Rubrics Self-Evolving for Better Supervision

While the two-role design enables a single policy model to self-propose rubrics for CoT supervision, we argue that such rubrics may get saturated: once consistently satisfied by all rollouts, they lose discriminative power over CoT quality and no longer provide reward signals, as shown in Section 4.3.
Therefore, we further propose to self-evolve the rubric generation capability, so as to progressively produce more informative and challenging rubrics that mitigate saturation.

Self-evolving with validity reward. To achieve rubricator self-evolving, we reward the fraction of the number of valid rubrics among all generated rubrics as follows: [...] We show that self-proposed and self-evolving rubrics provide reliable CoT supervision signals even without outcome rewards, enabling RLCER to outperform outcome-centric RLVR.
Moreover, when used as in-prompt hints, these self-proposed rubrics further improve inference-time performance. [...] ### 4.1 Key Idea

Refer to caption

The key idea of RLCER is to go beyond rewarding what LLMs answer (i.e., final-answer correctness) to explicitly and autonomously reward how LLMs think (i.e., CoT quality) using self-proposed, self-evolving rubrics. The rubrics are evolved by tracking how well their satisfaction correlates with the final answer correctness.

results.title: Paper page - Reinforcing Chain-of-Thought Reasoning with Self-Evolving Rubrics
results.content:
Hugging Face's logo

# Reinforcing Chain-of-Thought Reasoning with Self-Evolving Rubrics

## Abstract

RLCER enables autonomous chain-of-thought rewarding through self-evolving rubrics that provide reliable supervision signals without human annotation or outcome rewards. [...] Despite chain-of-thought (CoT) playing crucial roles in LLM reasoning, directly rewarding it is difficult: training a reward model demands heavy human labeling efforts, and static RMs struggle with evolving CoT distributions and reward hacking. These challenges motivate us to seek an autonomous CoT rewarding approach that requires no human annotation efforts and can evolve gradually. Inspired by recent self-evolving training methods, we propose RLCER (Reinforcement Learning with CoT Supervision via Self-Evolving Rubrics), which enhances the outcome-centric RLVR by rewarding CoTs with self-proposed and self-evolving rubrics. We show that self-proposed and self-evolving rubrics provide reliable CoT supervision signals even without outcome rewards, enabling RLCER to outperform [...] even without outcome rewards, enabling RLCER to outperform outcome-centric RLVR. Moreover, when used as in-prompt hints, these self-proposed rubrics further improve inference-time performance.

results.title: SkillCoach: Self-Evolving Rubrics for Enhancing Agentic Skill-Use
results.content: Introducing the SkillCoach framework that helps giant language model (LLM) agents effectively utilize complex technology libraries. The existing method of simply judging the success of the final result has the limitation of not being able to capture the problem of the agent accidentally getting the right answer or going through the wrong process. To address this, SkillCoach creates a self-evolving rubric that evaluates the agent's course of conduct in four dimensions: skill selection, procedural compliance, skill combination, and result reflection. According to research, the system significantly improves the agent's proficiency by accurately diagnosing the agent's potential failure points and selecting high-quality learning data. As a result, this framework ensures that agents operate [...] in the correct sequential order. And finally, skill grounded reflection. This is effectively analyzing intermediate artifacts and outputs to adjust the trajectory mid-flight. These four pillars form the total meta ability matrix, and failing at literally any one of them compromises the entire agentic loop. Next up, section four, the self-evolving rubric pipeline. To calibrate autonomously, the pipeline extracts highly visible trajectory evidence. We're talking about distinct file edits, document reads, and specific parameter tool calls. It then proposes targeted arbitration patches based purely on this calibration data. And this highlights the systems absolute stability because it utilizes a strict hard gate. The evolution is kept at a maximum of six rounds and automatically halts if [...] As a result, this framework ensures that agents operate more reliably even in large environments, such as enterprise libraries.
